explanations.1: Fluids, which include liquids and gases, are defined by their inability to resist shearing forces, allowing them to flow.
explanations.2: The large amount of empty space between gas molecules allows them to be forced closer together under pressure.
explanations.3: Liquids have a fixed volume due to close atomic contact but lack a rigid shape because atoms can move past one another.
explanations.4: Volume $V = \text{Area} \times \text{depth} = 50.0 \times 10^6\text{ m}^2 \times 40.0\text{ m} = 2.00 \times 10^9\text{ m}^3$; Mass $m = \rho V = 1000 \times 2.00 \times 10^9 = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\text{ kg}$.
explanations.5: Density is defined as mass divided by volume, so a substance with lower density requires more volume to reach the same mass.
explanations.6: Force $F = PA = P(\pi r^2) = (6.90 \times 10^6\text{ Pa})(3.14)(0.075\text{ m})^2 = 1.22 \times 10^5\text{ N}$.
explanations.7: Pressure is a scalar that acts in all directions within a fluid and always exerts force perpendicular to the surface of an object.
explanations.8: Pressure is force divided by area ($P=F/A$), so a smaller area of contact results in higher pressure for the same force.
explanations.9: Pressure due to depth is calculated using $P = h\rho g = (10.0\text{ m})(1000\text{ kg/m}^3)(9.80\text{ m/s}^2) = 9.80 \times 10^4\text{ Pa}$.
explanations.10: Pressure depends only on depth ($h\rho g$), and force is the average pressure times the area of contact ($F = \bar{P}A$).
explanations.11: Atmospheric pressure is the result of the weight of the air column above a given point; higher altitudes have shorter air columns.
explanations.12: Pascal's principle states that pressure changes in an enclosed fluid are distributed equally throughout the entire fluid and container walls.
explanations.14: Hydraulic systems can multiply force, but they must obey the law of conservation of energy; the distance moved by the output piston is smaller.
explanations.15: Absolute pressure is the sum of gauge pressure and atmospheric pressure ($P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$).
explanations.16: Since absolute pressure cannot be negative, the gauge pressure reaches its minimum when absolute pressure is zero ($P_g = 0 - P_{atm}$).
explanations.17: Mercury is 13.6 times denser than water, meaning an atmospheric column is ~760mm rather than ~10m high.
explanations.18: Systolic pressure is the maximum pressure in the arteries when the heart beats, typically measured in mm Hg.
explanations.19: Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces.
explanations.20: An object sinks if its average density is greater than that of the fluid, making its weight greater than the maximum buoyant force.
explanations.21: Specific gravity is the ratio of the density of the substance to the density of water ($2700 / 1000 = 2.7$).
explanations.22: The fraction submerged for a floating object is the ratio of the object's density to the fluid's density ($970 / 1000 = 0.97$ or $97\%$).
explanations.23: The buoyant force equals the apparent weight loss ($100\text{ N} - 80\text{ N} = 20\text{ N}$).
explanations.24: Surface tension arises because molecules at the surface are pulled together by neighbors below and to the side, but not above.
explanations.25: Adhesion refers to the attraction between unlike molecules, such as water and glass.
explanations.26: For a spherical bubble with two surfaces (inside and out), the gauge pressure is $4\gamma / r$.
explanations.27: When the contact angle is less than 90 degrees, adhesive forces are strong enough to overcome cohesion and pull the liquid up.
explanations.28: Surfactants reduce surface tension in the alveoli, making it possible for the lungs to inflate.
explanations.29: $18\text{ mm Hg}$ is ~ $2400\text{ Pa}$. $h = P/(\rho g) = 2400 / (1000 \times 9.8) \approx 0.24\text{ m}$.
explanations.30: Transpiration creates negative pressure, and the strong cohesive forces between water molecules pull the sap up in a continuous chain.
